summary: This is a brief link post where Joel Spolsky highlights two notable observations from other writers. David Pogue criticizes LG's poorly designed control system that requires seven warnings in the manual, implying the design itself is flawed. Eric Sink challenges the conventional wisdom around word-of-mouth marketing, arguing that most advice simply states that buzz is good without explaining how to generate it. Joel curates these links without additional commentary, letting the quotes speak for themselves.
key_points: If a product's interface requires extensive warnings in the manual, the design itself needs to be reconsidered rather than documented around
Most marketing advice about word-of-mouth is circular — it explains why buzz is good but not how to create it
Eric Sink draws an analogy between buzz advice and telling people what rich people can buy — stating obvious outcomes without explaining how to achieve them
money_quotes: Listen up, LG dudes: I'm sorry, but if your primary control system requires seven warnings in your manual, maybe you should reconsider your system.
Everybody knows how powerful word-of-mouth and buzz can be.
This is a little bit like explaining the nice things that rich people can buy. It is obvious that good results are good. We don't need to be told that.
What we need to know is how to make those good results happen.
key_insight: Good design shouldn't need extensive warnings, and good advice shouldn't just tell you that good outcomes are desirable — both should focus on how to get things right.
